Dissolution data: All assets (ex-cash) realised for ₹9,75,000; All liabilities settled for ₹2,90,000 (book ₹3,00,000); Dissolution expenses ₹15,000; Cash and bank ₹25,000; Non-cash assets at book value ₹10,50,000; Partners' Capitals: L ₹3,00,000; M ₹2,50,000; N ₹2,10,000; Partners' Loans: M ₹50,000; N ₹30,000. What is M's total entitlement?
questions.explanation: Asset loss = Rs.10,50,000 - Rs.9,75,000 = Rs.75,000. Liability saving = Rs.3,00,000 - Rs.2,90,000 = Rs.10,000. Expenses = Rs.15,000. Net realisation loss = Rs.75,000 - Rs.10,000 + Rs.15,000 = Rs.80,000. M's share in 3:2:1 is 2/6, so M bears Rs.26,667. M's capital after loss = Rs.2,50,000 - Rs.26,667 = Rs.2,23,333. Add M's loan Rs.50,000: total entitlement = Rs.2,73,333. Option C is correct.

questions.question: A common error in dissolution accounting is: Transfer of ₹50,000 Workmen Compensation Reserve (with no pending claims) to Realisation Account instead of Partners' Capital Accounts. What is the financial impact of this error?
options: No impact — the final result is the same either way
Realisation profit overstated by ₹50,000; Partners' Capital Accounts understated by ₹50,000 each (proportional)
The error causes double counting — Realisation A/c shows ₹50,000 extra credit; Partners' Capitals are not credited the ₹50,000 directly
Partners receive ₹50,000 less in total — cash distribution error
questions.explanation: Correct treatment: WCR (no claims) should go to Partners' Capital Accounts directly. Error: WCR ₹50,000 is credited to Realisation Account instead. Impact: (1) Realisation Account is overstated by ₹50,000 on credit side → Realisation shows ₹50,000 extra 'profit' from WCR. (2) This extra ₹50,000 profit IS then transferred to partners in PSR — so partners still get ₹50,000 eventually, but only in PSR (not necessarily their share). (3) The Realisation Account is inflated — it misrepresents the actual profit/loss on realisation. This is a conceptual error affecting the quality of reporting, though the final distribution to partners may ultimately be the same (if PSR equals the correct distribution ratio).

questions.question: Partners A, B, C (A:B:C = 5:4:1) dissolve their firm. Realisation A/c is balanced as follows: Total Debit = ₹X; Total Credit = ₹X + ₹30,000 (profit). After profit transfer: A's Capital ₹4,65,000; B's Capital ₹3,52,000; C's Capital ₹88,000. Before profit transfer, A's Capital was ₹4,50,000. What was B's Capital before profit transfer?
questions.explanation: Realisation profit = ₹30,000. PSR 5:4:1, denominator 10. A's share = 5/10 × ₹30,000 = ₹15,000. A's Capital before = ₹4,65,000 − ₹15,000 = ₹4,50,000 ✓ (matches given data). B's share = 4/10 × ₹30,000 = ₹12,000. B's Capital before profit = ₹3,52,000 − ₹12,000 = ₹3,40,000. ✓ (Option A). C's share = 1/10 × ₹30,000 = ₹3,000. C's Capital before = ₹88,000 − ₹3,000 = ₹85,000 (check). All consistent.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): When a firm is dissolved, all entries in the Realisation Account affect Partners' Capital Accounts indirectly through the profit or loss on realisation.
Reason (R): Partners' Capital Accounts are only affected by the net profit or loss of the Realisation Account, not by individual asset or liability entries within it.
options: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct
questions.explanation: This is a subtle but important point: All individual asset and liability entries in Realisation A/c DO NOT directly affect Partners' Capital Accounts. Only the NET result (profit or loss) of the Realisation Account is transferred to Partners' Capital Accounts. Exception: when a partner takes over an asset — this DIRECTLY debits the partner's capital (not through the net result). However, in the standard sense, A is correct that Realisation entries affect capitals through the net result, and R correctly explains this mechanism. Both are correct and R explains A in the typical scenario.

questions.question: A, B, C (2:2:1) dissolve their firm. After realisation, the Realisation Account shows a loss of Rs.2,50,000. Partners' Capitals before realisation loss are: A Rs.3,00,000; B Rs.2,00,000; C Rs.50,000. What is B's final capital position after transferring the realisation loss?
options: B's Capital = Rs.1,00,000
B's Capital = Rs.75,000
B's final capital = Rs.50,000
B's final capital = Rs.0
questions.explanation: The loss of Rs.2,50,000 is shared in the 2:2:1 ratio. B's share = 2/5 x Rs.2,50,000 = Rs.1,00,000. B's final capital = Rs.2,00,000 - Rs.1,00,000 = Rs.1,00,000. C's share is Rs.50,000, so C's capital becomes zero rather than negative; no deficiency adjustment is needed. Option A is correct.

questions.question: A firm has the following data at dissolution: Book value of all non-cash assets ₹20,00,000; Amount realised ₹17,50,000; External liabilities (book) ₹4,00,000; Amount paid to settle liabilities ₹3,75,000; Dissolution expenses ₹25,000; Opening cash ₹50,000. How much cash is available to pay partners (after all obligations met)?
questions.explanation: Cash flows: Inflows — Opening cash ₹50,000 + Realised from assets ₹17,50,000 = ₹18,00,000. Outflows — Liabilities paid ₹3,75,000 + Expenses ₹25,000 = ₹4,00,000. Net cash available for partners = ₹18,00,000 − ₹4,00,000 = ₹14,00,000. ✓ (Option A). This cash will be distributed: First to partners' loans, then to partners' capitals in the settled amounts after accounting for realisation loss of ₹2,50,000 − ₹25,000 + ₹25,000 = ₹2,50,000 asset loss minus ₹25,000 saving = ₹2,50,000 loss + ₹25,000 expenses = total loss shared among partners.

questions.question: Partners X, Y, Z (2:2:1) dissolve their firm. Realisation Account profit = ₹75,000. X's Capital ₹5,00,000; Y's Capital ₹4,00,000; Z's Capital ₹2,00,000. Loans: X's Loan ₹1,00,000; Z's Loan ₹50,000. Cash available = ₹12,45,000. Which of the following distributions is CORRECT?
options: Loans first; then capitals: X's Loan ₹1,00,000; Z's Loan ₹50,000; X's Capital ₹5,30,000; Y's Capital ₹4,30,000; Z's Capital ₹2,15,000; Total ₹13,25,000 ≠ ₹12,45,000
Profit share: X ₹30,000; Y ₹30,000; Z ₹15,000. Capitals: X ₹5,30,000; Y ₹4,30,000; Z ₹2,15,000. With loans: total = ₹13,25,000; available ₹12,45,000 → shortfall ₹80,000 — something wrong in data
questions.explanation: Profit shares (2:2:1): X = 2/5 × ₹75,000 = ₹30,000; Y = ₹30,000; Z = ₹15,000. Updated capitals: X = ₹5,30,000; Y = ₹4,30,000; Z = ₹2,15,000. Total claims: X (₹1,00,000 + ₹5,30,000) + Y (₹4,30,000) + Z (₹50,000 + ₹2,15,000) = ₹6,30,000 + ₹4,30,000 + ₹2,65,000 = ₹13,25,000. Given cash = ₹12,45,000, shortfall = ₹80,000. This indicates either the problem has inconsistent data OR there are external creditors still unpaid from the ₹12,45,000. Option C correctly identifies the discrepancy.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): If a firm's assets were completely sold at book value and all liabilities settled exactly at book value, the Realisation Account would show zero balance before accounting for dissolution expenses.
Reason (R): The Realisation Account profit/loss reflects the deviation of actual realisations from book values; at exact book values, all debit and credit entries would perfectly offset.
options: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct
questions.explanation: If ALL assets are sold at EXACTLY book value: Debit (assets at book) = Credit (proceeds = book value) for each asset → net ₹0 per asset. If ALL liabilities settled at EXACTLY book value: Debit (payments) = Credit (liabilities at book) for each → net ₹0 per liability. Combined: Debit total = Credit total → Realisation balance = ₹0 before dissolution expenses. R correctly explains A: the balance of Realisation Account represents the aggregate deviation from book values. When all transactions are exactly at book value, there is no deviation — hence zero balance. Dissolution expenses would then create the only final loss.

questions.question: P, Q, R are partners in 3:2:1. At dissolution: Realisation A/c Debit total = ₹X; Realisation A/c Credit total = ₹X; Profit/Loss = ₹0. But: Dissolution expenses of ₹18,000 are still to be entered (paid from cash). After this entry, what is the final impact on Partners' Capitals?
options: Each partner bears loss in 3:2:1 ratio → P ₹9,000; Q ₹6,000; R ₹3,000
Expenses create ₹18,000 loss equally → each bears ₹6,000
No impact as Realisation was balanced
Expenses of ₹18,000 borne by the partner who incurred them
questions.explanation: Before dissolution expenses, Realisation Account was balanced (profit/loss = ₹0). After recording dissolution expenses: Debit Realisation A/c ₹18,000; Credit Cash A/c ₹18,000. This creates a ₹18,000 LOSS in the Realisation Account. This loss is then transferred to Partners' Capital Accounts in their profit-sharing ratio (3:2:1): P = 3/6 × ₹18,000 = ₹9,000 (Dr); Q = 2/6 × ₹6,000 (Dr); R = 1/6 × ₹3,000 (Dr). Each partner's capital reduces by their respective share. ✓ (Option A).

questions.question: On dissolution of firm (A:B = 3:1), the Realisation Account is prepared. After closing Realisation A/c, A's Capital = ₹9,60,000 (Cr); B's Capital = ₹2,00,000 (Cr). A's Loan to firm = ₹1,50,000. Bank balance = ₹13,10,000. In what sequence and amount is cash distributed?
options: Step 1: A's Loan ₹1,50,000; Step 2: A's Capital ₹9,60,000; Step 3: B's Capital ₹2,00,000; Total ₹13,10,000 ✓
Step 1: B's Capital ₹2,00,000; Step 2: A's Loan ₹1,50,000; Step 3: A's Capital ₹9,60,000; Total ₹13,10,000
A gets ₹11,10,000 (loan + capital) first; B gets ₹2,00,000
Both partners receive capitals simultaneously; loans paid after capitals
questions.explanation: Correct sequence of cash distribution on dissolution: STEP 1 — External creditors (already settled). STEP 2 — Partners' Loans: A's Loan ₹1,50,000. STEP 3 — Partners' Capitals: A's Capital ₹9,60,000; B's Capital ₹2,00,000. Total = ₹1,50,000 + ₹9,60,000 + ₹2,00,000 = ₹13,10,000 = Bank balance ✓. Option A correctly states this sequence. Option C is practically the same outcome for A (₹11,10,000 total) but the sequence matters — loans MUST be paid before capitals, not simultaneously.

questions.question: On dissolution, Realisation Account shows loss ₹72,000. Partners X and Y share profits in 5:4. What journal entry is passed to close Realisation Account?
options: Dr Realisation A/c ₹72,000; Cr X's Capital ₹40,000; Cr Y's Capital ₹32,000
Dr X's Capital ₹40,000; Dr Y's Capital ₹32,000; Cr Realisation A/c ₹72,000
Dr X's Capital ₹36,000; Dr Y's Capital ₹36,000; Cr Realisation A/c ₹72,000
Dr Realisation A/c ₹72,000; Cr P&L A/c ₹72,000
questions.explanation: Realisation Account shows a DEBIT balance (Loss) of ₹72,000. To close this: the loss is transferred to Partners' Capital Accounts (debit — reducing capitals) in profit-sharing ratio 5:4. X's share = 5/9 × ₹72,000 = ₹40,000; Y's share = 4/9 × ₹72,000 = ₹32,000. Journal entry: Debit X's Capital A/c ₹40,000; Debit Y's Capital A/c ₹32,000; Credit Realisation A/c ₹72,000. This closes the Realisation Account and reduces partners' capital balances by their shares of the loss.

questions.question: At dissolution of firm RS (R:S = 2:3), Realisation A/c shows: Assets (Dr) Rs.12,00,000; Liabilities paid (Dr) Rs.2,80,000; Liabilities transferred (Cr) Rs.3,00,000; Assets realised (Cr) Rs.10,80,000; Expenses (Dr) Rs.30,000. R's Capital Rs.4,20,000; S's Capital Rs.4,50,000. Both partners have no loans outstanding. Available cash for capital settlement = Rs.7,40,000. Calculate each partner's final settlement.
questions.explanation: Asset loss = Rs.12,00,000 - Rs.10,80,000 = Rs.1,20,000. Liability saving = Rs.3,00,000 - Rs.2,80,000 = Rs.20,000. Expenses = Rs.30,000. Net realisation loss = Rs.1,20,000 - Rs.20,000 + Rs.30,000 = Rs.1,30,000. R bears 2/5 = Rs.52,000 and S bears 3/5 = Rs.78,000. R's settlement = Rs.4,20,000 - Rs.52,000 = Rs.3,68,000. S's settlement = Rs.4,50,000 - Rs.78,000 = Rs.3,72,000. Total = Rs.7,40,000, matching available cash. Option A is correct.

questions.question: On dissolution, a firm discovers that a creditor of ₹20,000 has already been paid (before dissolution proceedings began) but the liability still appears in the Balance Sheet by mistake. The dissolution team pays the creditor again (₹20,000). What is the consequence in Realisation Account?
options: Creditor credited ₹20,000 to Realisation; actual payment ₹20,000 debited — net ₹0 in Realisation; error has no financial impact
Creditor credited ₹20,000 to Realisation; payment ₹20,000 debited — Realisation result unaffected; but ₹20,000 overpaid — partners bear the loss
Duplicate payment creates ₹20,000 extra debit in Realisation; partners ultimately bear this loss through reduced capitals
Creditor was already paid; Realisation A/c should NOT have credited creditor; duplicate payment ₹20,000 creates loss; partners bear ₹20,000 loss
questions.explanation: What happens: (1) Creditor ₹20,000 (incorrectly in Balance Sheet) is transferred to Credit of Realisation A/c. (2) The creditor is paid ₹20,000 again (duplicate payment): Debit Realisation ₹20,000; Credit Cash ₹20,000. Net effect in Realisation: Credit ₹20,000 (liability transferred) − Debit ₹20,000 (payment) = ₹0 net impact from these entries alone. BUT the CASH has been paid twice — ₹20,000 extra out of firm's funds. This ₹20,000 extra expenditure effectively reduces cash available for partners. The Realisation A/c entries cancel each other, but the CASH impact is a real loss — ultimately shared by partners. Option C best describes this: there's an effective ₹20,000 extra debit (through cash drain) affecting partners.

questions.question: On dissolution, firm GH (G:H = 3:2) has a Hire Purchase Liability of ₹40,000 remaining. The asset (machinery) acquired under HP is book value ₹80,000 (net after depreciation). HP liability is treated as external liability. Machinery is sold for ₹60,000. What is the net impact on Realisation Account from these two items?
options: Net gain ₹20,000
Net loss ₹20,000
Net gain ₹20,000 — no loss since HP liability saves the firm ₹40,000 — incorrect, already an obligation
Net impact ₹0
questions.explanation: Hire Purchase Liability of Rs.40,000 is an external liability and is transferred to the credit of Realisation. Machinery book value Rs.80,000 is transferred to the debit of Realisation. Machinery sold for Rs.60,000 is credited to Realisation. HP liability paid at Rs.40,000 is debited to Realisation. Debits = Rs.80,000 + Rs.40,000 = Rs.1,20,000. Credits = Rs.40,000 + Rs.60,000 = Rs.1,00,000. Net impact is a Realisation loss of Rs.20,000. Option B is correct.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): On dissolution, dissolution expenses agreed to be borne by a partner should always be recorded at the agreed amount (not actual) in Realisation Account.
Reason (R): The agreed amount determines the firm's charge — if actual is lower, the partner personally benefits; if higher, the partner personally absorbs the excess.
options: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct
questions.explanation: When a partner agrees to bear dissolution expenses at a fixed amount: The Realisation Account is debited at the AGREED AMOUNT (not actual): Dr Realisation A/c (agreed); Cr Partner's Capital A/c (agreed). The partner pays actual expenses: Dr Partner's Capital A/c (actual); Cr Cash A/c (actual). If actual < agreed: partner benefits (the credit to capital exceeds the debit for actual payment). If actual > agreed: partner absorbs the excess personally (pays more than compensated). R correctly explains A: the agreed amount is the firm's contractual obligation to the partner, regardless of actual expenditure. Both A and R are correct and aligned.

questions.question: A partnership dissolves. The partners (E:F = 1:3) share profits and losses. After all Realisation Account entries and transfer of profit/loss, E's Capital = ₹50,000 (Cr); F's Capital = ₹1,50,000 (Cr). E's Loan to firm = ₹25,000. F's Loan to firm = ₹1,00,000. Total cash = ₹3,25,000. Verify and describe final distribution.
options: Both A and B describe the same correct answer — total = ₹3,25,000 ✓
Total claims exceed ₹3,25,000 — cash insufficient
questions.explanation: Total claims: E's Loan ₹25,000 + E's Capital ₹50,000 + F's Loan ₹1,00,000 + F's Capital ₹1,50,000 = ₹3,25,000. Cash available = ₹3,25,000. ✓ Exactly balanced! Options A and B both describe the same truth: total claims = ₹3,25,000 = Cash available. The order of payment: First partner loans (E's Loan ₹25,000; F's Loan ₹1,00,000 — total ₹1,25,000); Then partner capitals (E ₹50,000; F ₹1,50,000 — total ₹2,00,000). Grand total = ₹3,25,000 ✓. Both A and B are correct — option C.

questions.question: A firm's Realisation Account is balanced at zero (no profit/loss). However, it is later found that a debtor of ₹8,000 (fully written off earlier — NOT in Balance Sheet) has paid ₹8,000. The Realisation Account was already closed. How should this be handled?
options: Reopen Realisation A/c; record ₹8,000; redistribute to partners
Cash ₹8,000 received directly distributed to partners in PSR without reopening Realisation A/c
Debit Bank A/c ₹8,000; Credit Partners' Capital Accounts in PSR — no need to reopen Realisation A/c
Both B and C are correct — same practical effect
questions.explanation: Since the debtor was already written off (not in Balance Sheet) and Realisation Account is closed, the ₹8,000 recovery is a windfall gain after dissolution proceedings. There are two acceptable approaches: (B) Distribute ₹8,000 cash directly to partners in PSR — practical approach; (C) Debit Bank ₹8,000; Credit Partners' Capital Accounts in PSR — accounting approach. Both achieve the same result: partners receive their PSR share of the ₹8,000 recovery. The Realisation Account need NOT be reopened for this post-closure item. Options B and C describe the same practical outcome → Option D is correct.

questions.question: Partners A, B, C (PSR 4:3:3) dissolve. On dissolution date: General Reserve ₹30,000; P&L (Dr) ₹10,000; Capitals: A ₹5,00,000; B ₹4,00,000; C ₹2,50,000. Realisation profit = ₹1,00,000. A's Loan ₹80,000. What is C's capital AFTER all transfers (reserves + realisation) but BEFORE cash payment?
questions.explanation: C's share in the 4:3:3 ratio is 3/10. C receives General Reserve share = 3/10 x Rs.30,000 = Rs.9,000. C bears P&L debit balance = 3/10 x Rs.10,000 = Rs.3,000. C receives realisation profit share = 3/10 x Rs.1,00,000 = Rs.30,000. Net addition = Rs.9,000 - Rs.3,000 + Rs.30,000 = Rs.36,000. C's capital after all transfers = Rs.2,50,000 + Rs.36,000 = Rs.2,86,000. Option A is correct.

questions.question: On dissolution, which of the following correctly describes a 'comprehensive' Realisation Account with ALL possible entries on each side?
options: DEBIT: Assets (net BV), Cash paid to creditors, Dissolution expenses, Unrecorded liabilities paid. CREDIT: Liabilities (book), Cash from asset sales, Partners' capitals for assets taken, Unrecorded assets realised, Provisions for bad debts.
DEBIT: All assets including cash, All liabilities, Expenses. CREDIT: All realised amounts.
DEBIT: Assets, Expenses, Liabilities paid, Unrecorded liabilities. CREDIT: Liabilities, Realised amounts, Provisions, Unrecorded assets, Partners' capitals for assets taken.
DEBIT: Only assets and expenses. CREDIT: Only liabilities and realised amounts.
questions.explanation: A COMPLETE Realisation Account contains: DEBIT SIDE: (1) Non-cash assets at net book value; (2) Cash paid to settle liabilities (actual); (3) Dissolution expenses; (4) Unrecorded liability payments; (5) Loss on realisation (if Cr > Dr, this goes to credit side). CREDIT SIDE: (1) External liabilities at book value; (2) Provisions (Bad Debts, Depreciation if handled separately); (3) Cash/Bank received from selling assets; (4) Partners' Capital A/c for assets taken over at agreed value; (5) Unrecorded assets realised; (6) Profit on realisation (if Dr > Cr, goes to debit side as 'transferred to capitals'). Option C most completely captures this.

questions.question: A, B, C are partners sharing profits in 3:2:1. Realisation Account: Debit ₹30,00,000; Credit ₹27,00,000. Partners' Loans: A ₹2,00,000; C ₹1,00,000. Partners' Capitals: A ₹8,00,000; B ₹6,00,000; C ₹4,00,000. After paying external creditors, cash = ₹17,50,000. Verify that cash distribution is correct.
options: Total claims after loss: A Loan Rs.2,00,000 + Capital Rs.6,50,000 + B Capital Rs.5,00,000 + C Loan Rs.1,00,000 + Capital Rs.3,50,000 = Rs.18,00,000, which does not match cash Rs.17,50,000
Total claims = Rs.17,50,000: A Loan Rs.2,00,000 + A Capital Rs.6,50,000 + B Capital Rs.5,00,000 + C Loan Rs.1,00,000 + C Capital Rs.3,00,000
Cash surplus is Rs.50,000
questions.explanation: Realisation loss = Rs.30,00,000 - Rs.27,00,000 = Rs.3,00,000. In the 3:2:1 ratio, A bears Rs.1,50,000, B bears Rs.1,00,000, and C bears Rs.50,000. Updated capitals are A Rs.6,50,000, B Rs.5,00,000, and C Rs.3,50,000. Total claims = A loan Rs.2,00,000 + A capital Rs.6,50,000 + B capital Rs.5,00,000 + C loan Rs.1,00,000 + C capital Rs.3,50,000 = Rs.18,00,000. Since cash is Rs.17,50,000, the stated cash distribution is short by Rs.50,000 and is not fully verified. Option A is correct.

questions.question: Partners X, Y (X:Y = 3:2) dissolve their firm. The Realisation Account shows: All entries completed; Profit = ₹50,000. X's Capital ₹6,00,000; Y's Capital ₹4,50,000. X's Loan ₹1,00,000; Y's Loan ₹50,000. Bank balance after external creditors = ₹12,30,000. Determine if the bank balance is exactly sufficient.
questions.explanation: Profit shares: X = 3/5 × ₹50,000 = ₹30,000; Y = 2/5 × ₹20,000. Updated capitals: X = ₹6,00,000 + ₹30,000 = ₹6,30,000; Y = ₹4,50,000 + ₹20,000 = ₹4,70,000. Total claims: X Loan ₹1,00,000 + X Capital ₹6,30,000 + Y Loan ₹50,000 + Y Capital ₹4,70,000 = ₹12,50,000. Bank = ₹12,30,000. Deficit = ₹20,000. For exact balance (option B/D): Y gets ₹5,00,000 instead of ₹5,20,000 — implies Y's Loan = ₹50,000 + Capital = ₹4,50,000 = ₹5,00,000 (before profit). But after profit Y = ₹4,70,000 capital. ₹50,000 + ₹4,70,000 = ₹5,20,000, not ₹5,00,000. Option B says Y gets ₹5,00,000 which is pre-profit. For exact balance ₹12,30,000: X ₹7,30,000 + Y ₹5,00,000 = ₹12,30,000 ✓ but Y's profit share seemingly not added.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The sequence of dissolution — distributing reserves, preparing Realisation Account, settling creditors, repaying loans, returning capitals — ensures that partners receive their fair share without prejudicing external creditors.
Reason (R): External creditors have first claim on firm assets; partners' interests (reserves, capitals) are secondary to these obligations.
options: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct
questions.explanation: Both A and R are correct. The sequence of dissolution does protect external creditors by settling them first. R correctly states that external creditors have the highest priority claim. However, R does NOT fully explain WHY the specific sequence (distributing reserves first, then Realisation Account, then payments) achieves this protection. The sequence is a procedural framework — distributing reserves to partner capitals first doesn't prejudice creditors because it just re-classifies amounts already owed to partners; external creditors are protected by the law and the order of payment (not just the accounting sequence). Hence, R is correct but is NOT the complete/correct explanation of A — option B.

questions.question: On dissolution, a firm (A:B:C = 3:2:1) has the following outstanding: Realisation profit ₹60,000; Goodwill (book ₹40,000) sold for ₹50,000 (already in realisation profit above); Patent ₹20,000 — unsold (loss included in profit figure above); A's Capital ₹4,80,000; B's Capital ₹3,60,000; C's Capital ₹2,40,000. If A had also agreed to pay dissolution expenses of ₹18,000 (firm to bear — agreed amount) and actual expenses = ₹18,000, what is A's final capital?
questions.explanation: The stated Realisation profit of Rs.60,000 already includes the goodwill gain and patent loss. Since A agreed to pay firm-borne dissolution expenses of Rs.18,000 and the actual expenses are also Rs.18,000, A is credited for the agreed amount and debited when paying it, so the net effect on A's capital is nil. A's share of profit in the 3:2:1 ratio is 3/6 x Rs.60,000 = Rs.30,000. A's final capital = Rs.4,80,000 + Rs.30,000 = Rs.5,10,000. Option A is correct.

questions.question: On dissolution of firm PQR (2:1:1), the Realisation Account entries show: Total Debit ₹40,00,000; Total Credit ₹38,40,000. After profit/loss transfer: P's Capital ₹9,60,000; Q's Capital ₹7,20,000; R's Capital ₹4,40,000. P's Loan ₹2,00,000; Available cash = ₹23,20,000. Which option shows the COMPLETE final distribution?
options: Both A and B are correct — same amounts, different presentation
questions.explanation: Verify: Loss = ₹40,00,000 − ₹38,40,000 = ₹1,60,000. Loss shares: P (2/4) = ₹80,000; Q (1/4) = ₹40,000; R (1/4) = ₹40,000. Capitals given are after loss transfer: P ₹9,60,000; Q ₹7,20,000; R ₹4,40,000. Payments: P's Loan ₹2,00,000 + P's Capital ₹9,60,000 + Q's Capital ₹7,20,000 + R's Capital ₹4,40,000 = ₹23,20,000 ✓. Option A: P's Loan ₹2,00,000 + ₹9,60,000 + Q ₹7,20,000 + R ₹4,40,000 = ₹23,20,000 ✓. Option B: P gets ₹11,60,000 (loan + capital) + Q + R = ₹23,20,000 ✓. Both A and B present the same correct distribution — Option C.

questions.question: A comprehensive dissolution scenario: Partners A, B, C (3:3:4) dissolve. Balance Sheet: Assets (ex-cash) ₹50,00,000; Cash ₹5,00,000; External Liabilities ₹12,00,000; A's Loan ₹3,00,000; Capitals: A ₹14,00,000; B ₹12,00,000; C ₹14,00,000. Non-cash assets realised ₹44,00,000; Liabilities settled ₹11,00,000; Expenses ₹50,000. What is C's final cash entitlement?
questions.explanation: Asset loss = Rs.50,00,000 - Rs.44,00,000 = Rs.6,00,000. Liability saving = Rs.12,00,000 - Rs.11,00,000 = Rs.1,00,000. Expenses are Rs.50,000. Net realisation loss = Rs.6,00,000 - Rs.1,00,000 + Rs.50,000 = Rs.5,50,000. C's share in the 3:3:4 ratio is 4/10, so C bears Rs.2,20,000. C's final cash entitlement = Rs.14,00,000 - Rs.2,20,000 = Rs.11,80,000. Option A is correct.
